query: compare rust vs go concurrency models under the hood

results.title: ✨ Google AI Overview
results.snippet:
AI Overview
Rust and Go approach concurrency from fundamentally different philosophical and technical angles. Go is designed for simplicity and massive scale in I/O-bound applications, while Rust prioritizes performance and memory safety through its unique ownership system. 
Architectural Comparison
\n| Feature | Go | Rust |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Model | Stackful Coroutines (Goroutines) | Stackless Coroutines (Async/Await) |\n| Runtime | Heavy, built-in scheduler (M:N) | Minimal, pluggable executors (e.g., Tokio) |\n| Memory | Garbage Collected | Ownership/Borrowing system |\n| Yielding | Implicit (preemptive points) | Explicit (via .await) |\n| Threads | Virtual threads (kilobytes each) | OS threads (std) or Futures (async) |\n\n
Under the Hood: Go
Goroutines: These are "green threads" managed by the Go runtime rather than the OS. They start with tiny stacks (~2KB) that grow and shrink as needed, allowing a single machine to run millions of them efficiently.
M:N Scheduler: The Go runtime uses an M:N scheduler to map M goroutines onto N OS threads. It handles blocking system calls by moving other goroutines to different threads to keep the CPU busy.
CSP Philosophy: Based on "Communicating Sequential Processes," Go encourages using channels to pass data between goroutines rather than sharing memory. 
Under the Hood: Rust
Async/Await State Machines: Rust’s async model is "stackless". When you write an async function, the compiler transforms it into a state machine (a Future). These do not have their own stacks, making them extremely memory-efficient for high-density tasks.
Fearless Concurrency: Rust's "ownership and borrowing" rules are enforced at compile time. This prevents data races—where two threads try to modify the same memory at once—before the code ever runs.
Pluggable Runtime: Unlike Go, Rust has no built-in async scheduler. Developers choose a library like Tokio or async-std depending on whether they need a work-stealing multi-threaded executor or a single-threaded one. 
When to Use Which
Choose Go for web servers, microservices, and cloud-native tools where developer speed and "good enough" performance are the priority.
Choose Rust for high-performance systems, real-time processing, or complex concurrent logic where you need maximum efficiency and a guarantee of thread safety.
